description:
You are a performance-tuning engineer who has been asked to do a quick-and-dirty CPU / memory snapshot of a small “problem” workload that already lives in the system.  
All the work has to be done from a non-privileged shell in the home directory tree: **/home/user**.

Starting point that already exists on disk
1. Directory **/home/user/apps/**
2. File **/home/user/apps/cpu_hog.py** – a tight loop that keeps one CPU core busy.
   (You do NOT have to create this file; it is already there and is executable.)

Goal  
1. Launch two separate background instances of that Python program so that they start burning CPU.  
2. After they have been running for a short while, record a one-shot performance snapshot (timestamp, PID, command, user-level CPU %, and resident-set memory in KB) for every running *cpu_hog.py* process.  
3. Store the information in a single log file whose exact path and exact format must be followed so the automated checker can verify your work.  
4. Cleanly terminate the background workload when you are done.

Where the snapshot must be written
• Directory: **/home/user/profile_logs/**  
• File:      **/home/user/profile_logs/app_profile.log**

Log-file format – ***must match exactly, including case, delimiter and header wording***  
• Plain text, UTF-8.  
• First line is a fixed header:  
  TIMESTAMP|PID|CMD|CPU_PERCENT|RESIDENT_KB  
• Every following line is one observation of one running cpu_hog.py process, in the order you capture them.  
• Each data line has **five** pipe-separated fields:  

  1. TIMESTAMP – local time of the sample, formatted **YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS** (24-hour clock).  
  2. PID        – decimal PID of the process at sampling time.  
  3. CMD        – command name string that contains the text **cpu_hog.py**.  
  4. CPU_PERCENT – user-plus-kernel utilisation as a floating-point number (e.g. 99.7). Round to one decimal place if your tool allows it; otherwise leave as your tool prints it.  
  5. RESIDENT_KB – resident-set size in kibibytes, an integer (no decimals).  

Example (***illustrative only*** – your numbers will differ):  
TIMESTAMP|PID|CMD|CPU_PERCENT|RESIDENT_KB  
2024-04-11 15:12:40|11342|python /home/user/apps/cpu_hog.py|99.5|18204  
2024-04-11 15:12:40|11343|python /home/user/apps/cpu_hog.py|99.5|18192  

Success criteria checked by the automated test
1. The directory **/home/user/profile_logs/** exists.  
2. The file **/home/user/profile_logs/app_profile.log** exists and is world-readable (0644 or more permissive).  
3. The very first line of the file is exactly the header shown above.  
4. At least two additional (data) lines are present.  
5. Every data line matches this regular expression (no embedded spaces at the ends of fields):  
   ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}\|[0-9]+\|.*cpu_hog\.py.*\|[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?\|[0-9]+$  
6. After all is done, there must be **no live processes** still running whose command line contains *cpu_hog.py*.

Your task as the AI assistant
• Do everything necessary from a normal bash shell to satisfy the six success criteria above, **without** using super-user privileges.  
• You may use standard GNU/Linux user-space tools only (e.g. ps, awk, grep, top ‑b ‑n1, pidstat, etc.).  
• Remember to create the destination directory if it is missing and to give the log file the correct name and mode.  
• Make sure you spawn the workload, capture the snapshot, write the file in the required format, and then stop the workload so nothing is left running.  
• Checks will be run immediately after your shell session finishes, so make sure all artefacts are on disk by then.

The automated grader will look only at on-disk artefacts; console output is allowed but will not be graded.
truth:
Initial files that already exist before the agent starts
• /home/user/apps/cpu_hog.py  
  Contents (exact):  
  --------------------------------------------------  
  #!/usr/bin/env python3  
  import math, time  
  while True:  
      x = math.sqrt(98765.4321) ** 2      # pointless calculation  
  --------------------------------------------------  
  File mode is 0755 (rwxr-xr-x) so it can be executed directly.

What the tests will verify AFTER the agent finishes
1. Directory /home/user/profile_logs/ exists and is a directory.  
2. app_profile.log exists at /home/user/profile_logs/app_profile.log.  
3. File mode of app_profile.log is 0644 (owner writable, world readable) or less restrictive.  
4. app_profile.log first line == "TIMESTAMP|PID|CMD|CPU_PERCENT|RESIDENT_KB\n" (with an LF line ending).  
5. app_profile.log contains ≥ 3 total lines (header + ≥ 2 data lines).  
6. For every data line, the test will:  
   a. split on the "|" character into exactly 5 fields;  
   b. verify field-1 matches datetime pattern YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS;  
   c. verify field-2 is an integer ≥ 1;  
   d. verify field-3 (CMD) contains the substring "cpu_hog.py";  
   e. verify field-4 is a positive number (integer or x.y floating point);  
   f. verify field-5 is a positive integer.  
7. Using "pgrep -f cpu_hog.py", the test will confirm that no processes remain.
name: task_000000_3282064b
